text: The patient is positioned in the left lateral decubitus position, with three surgical ports visibly placed along the midline of the abdomen.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum lateral to the right posterior rectus sheath after reducing the hernia contents.

The surgeon begins dissection away from the hernia defect, carefully proceeding above the transversalis fascia to minimize tears in the peritoneum and ensure placement of the mesh in the extraperitoneal space.
The surgeon creates a large preperitoneal pocket, extending the dissection inferiorly towards the inguinal region and superiorly up to the subdiaphragmatic space.
The surgeon encounters the hernia sac and carefully dissects it away from the surrounding tissues, ensuring clear separation from adjacent anatomical structures.
The surgeon continues the dissection in a medial to lateral direction, reaching as far posterior as the paravertebral muscles, which are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon encounters fibrosis in the region, leading to unavoidable tears in the peritoneum during dissection.
The surgeon achieves adequate hemostasis in the surgical field before proceeding to close the defect primarily.
The surgeon observes a transverse defect measuring approximately sixteen centimeters wide by eighteen centimeters long.
The surgeon begins the primary closure of the transverse defect at the posterior aspect using a zero V-lock suture on a 180-degree needle, cut to twelve inches.
The surgeon encounters significant tension while closing the defect located between the tenth and eleventh ribs, due to the defect's size and position.
The surgeon takes the first several suture bites to begin re-approximating the edges of the transverse defect between the tenth and eleventh ribs, gradually tightening the suture to reduce tension.
The surgeon attempts to suture down the dome of the defect to reduce dead space, but the procedure results in distortion of the skin, preventing successful closure.
The surgeon places a second V-lock suture in the mid portion of the defect at its widest point, where the tension is visibly greatest.
The surgeon takes a few suture bites at the defect site, gently tightening the suture after each bite to approximate the tissue edges.
The surgeon redistributes tension across the entire width of the defect, facilitating the gradual closure of the area between the tenth and eleventh ribs.
The surgeon alternates between tightening the mid portion suture at the area of greatest tension and the lateral suture, adjusting both to redistribute tension across the defect.
The surgeon begins placing the third V-lock suture at the medial aspect of the defect, preparing to address the remaining tension in the area.
The surgeon takes and tightens individual suture bites in a low-tension area of the defect, progressively bringing the edges together for complete closure.
The surgeon places a fourth suture between the first two sutures to bridge the gap in the defect, ensuring the edges are brought closer together for closure.
The surgeon places a fourth suture to close the defect, utilizing overlapping bites to bolster the closure, aided by the re-approximation achieved by previous sutures.
A 16 by 12 centimeter self-gripping mesh is placed centered over the suture line to alleviate tension encountered during the closure of the defect.
The surgeon measures the preperitoneal space and places a 24 cm by 20 cm mid-weight microporous polypropylene mesh over the defect area.
The surgeon places the mesh with a trans-fascial suture at the medial aspect, then unrolls it from medial to lateral, ensuring it lies flat against the abdominal wall with all corners tucked in.

The surgeon places two additional trans-fascial sutures on the medial aspect of the mesh. An additional running V-lock suture is applied in the midline of the defect, centering the mesh over the repair site.
The surgeon applies fibrin glue to secure the mesh posteriorly, followed by the placement of several Tuo interrupted vicryl sutures to ensure the mesh lies flat against the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon closes peritoneal tears with vicryl sutures and secures the peritoneal flap using a running 3-0 V-lock horizontal mattress stitch, ensuring the mesh remains completely extra-peritoneal.
